rulings.Case Title: RICHARDS, DIANE, DBA SMOG QUEEN VS. BUREAU OF AUTOMOTIVE REPAIR
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Prosecute; Points And Authorities In Support; Declaration Of Justin R. Surber
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 7, 2025, Line 1, RESPONDENT BUREAU OF AUTOMOTIVE REPAIR's Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Prosecute. The unopposed motion to dismiss for delay in prosecution is granted. The petition is dismissed with prejudice. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for movant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA).

rulings.Case Title: ABRAHAM WOOD VS. JOSEPH BIRCH ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR LEAVE TO File Cross-Complaint Against Joseph Birch And Emma Anderson-Smith; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Pamela B. Bumatay
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 7, 2025, Line 3, DEFENDANT WATERTON RESIDENTIAL, L.L.C. (ERRONEOUSLY SUED AS WATERTON), WRPV XIV DH SF, L.L.C.'s MOTION FOR LEAVE TO File Cross-Complaint Against Joseph Birch And Emma Anderson-Smith. Waterton Residential, LLC and WRPV XIV DH SF, LLC's motion for leave to file a cross-complaint against Joseph Birch and Emma Anderson-Smith is granted. The parties cite inapposite authorities relating to the filing of a compulsory cross-complaint. (See, e.g., CCP 426.50; Silver Organizations Ltd. v. Frank (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 94.) The proposed cross-complaint for indemnity is a permissive cross-complaint subject to the requirements of CCP 428.10. "There are no compulsory cross-complaints against parties other than plaintiff." (The Rutter Group California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial 6:524 (2025).) The proposed cross-complaint arises out of the same transaction or controversy and therefore the filing is proper under CCP 428.10(b). While Waterton could have filed this motion earlier, Birch and Anderson-Smith are not prejudiced since they have been parties to this case since its inception and the trial is set for November 17, 2025. The court further notes that denying this motion would serve little purpose. Waterton would be able to commence an action for indemnity against Birch and Anderson-Smith if it were ultimately found liable to plaintiff. The dispute should be resolved in one action. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for movant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA).

rulings.Case Title: CASEY SULLIVAN VS. FORD MOTOR COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production Of Documents, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 7, 2025, Line 8, PLAINTIFF CASEY SULLIVAN's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production Of Documents, Set One. (Part 1 of 2). Plaintiff Casey Sullivan's motion to compel further responses to Sullivan's request for production of documents, set one, from Ford Motor Company is granted in part continued on the court's own motion to July 16, 2025. Ford has offered to produce documents identified in Exhibit A to Sullivan's meet and confer letter of December 27 on condition that it resolves all discovery requests in this set of RFPs. Sullivan responded to Ford's offer with a boilerplate meet and confer letter that did not address the offer at all or meaningfully attempt to narrow the dispute. Sullivan did not accept Ford's offer to telephonically meet and confer. Moreover, Sullivan does not explain in the moving papers to this court why Ford's offer of production was inadequate. A moving party seeking to compel further responses to a request for production of documents has the burden of setting forth specific facts showing good cause to justify the discovery sought by the demand. (CCP 2031.310(b)(1).) The court orders Sullivan to meet and confer telephonically or in person concerning the compromise Ford offered in its letter of January 15, 2024. The court is hopeful that the parties will resolve their outstanding discovery disputes. The motion is continued to July 16 unless it is withdrawn by Sullivan in the interim. Sullivan shall file a supplemental brief no later than nine court days in advance of the continued hearing date concerning what issues are still in dispute. Sullivan must also file an updated separate statement that shows specific facts and good cause to order production of any documents that remain in dispute. Boilerplate assertions of relevance will not suffice. Ford may respond by supplemental brief and updated separate statement by no later than five court days in advance of the hearing. CCP 2031.300(c) provides that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to a demand for inspection . . . unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." The failure to meet and confer is a misuse of the discovery process and burdens the court with reviewing disputes that the parties could resolve through good-faith efforts. (CCP 2023.010(i).) The court finds that Sullivan acted without substantial justification in failing to meet and confer and orders Sullivan to pay sanctions of $500 to the San Francisco Superior Court within 10 court days of entry of this order. (Continued to Part 2). =(301/CVA).
